topic: 2024/2025 Annual review of Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment (Continued)
description: Thursday 4 December 2025 | Economic Development Science and Innovation Committee 2024/2025 Annual review of Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment (Continued) Hearing of evidence: Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment Suzanne Stew, Acting Chief Executive Nic Blakeley, Deputy Secretary, Labour, Science and Enterprise Richard Griffiths, Deputy Secretary, Corporate and Digital Shared Services Alison McDonald, Deputy Secretary, Immigration Robert Pigou, Deputy Secretary, Regional Development & Commercial Services Melanie Porter, Deputy Secretary, Strategy and Assurance Paul Stocks, Deputy Secretary, Building, Resources and Markets Ingrid Bayliss, Acting Deputy Secretary, Te Whakatairanga Service Delivery Lesley McConnell, Chief Advisor to the Secretary Paul Helm, Chief Financial Officer Jeannie Melville, Deputy Chief Operating Officer Immigration Simon Thomas, Head of Building System Delivery & Assurance Andy Hume, General manager, Commerce, Consumer and Business Jivan Grewal, General Manager, Workplace Relations and Safety Policy Danette Olsen, General Manager, Science System Investment and Performance Fiona Whiteridge, General Manager, Refugee and Migrant Services Steve Watson, General Manager, Immigration Compliance and Investigations
descriptionSummary: 2024/2025 Annual review of Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment (Continued). Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment. Suzanne Stew, Acting Chief Executive

chunks.text: Yeah. Yeah, absolutely. Cutting up. Feel free. I think the software's impressive. I don't know what thing you're thinking about. Oh, cheers, Colby. Conference. That's gotta be nice once we get- Someone go have a seat over there. And it's got this weird thing too. Interesting. Oh, yeah. Could you ? Where? Oh, yeah. Ah, thank you, Suzanne. It's Oh, good. It's so good. Yeah.

That's a no? Thank you, everyone. So we resume, uh, BI Annual Review, and this is from part five. Uh, you settled? Thank you. You need time to settle down? You're good? Just getting some water (laughs) Okay. (laughs) Here.

Okay. Uh, Scott Bullas. Mm. Thank you. So look, I just want to return to the Healthy Home Standards and the compliance team that you've got in operation doing, doing compliance- (laughs). ... uh, in the Deep South, but not just in the Deep South. Um, one thing that has emerged from the wider sector is that there are already a group of people, or a cohort of people, who are doing Healthy Home Standard assessments or, um, or home performance advisory services.

Mm-hmm. So, um, thinking about how to make best use of ensuring compliance and to check the quality of our homes for well-being purposes, has, has MBIE given consideration to contracting out some of that service to those people who are already practicing, essentially practicing that? Um, and have you given any thought to how that might, how that might work? I do know that there's been some approaches to government by, like Community Energy Network, for example, who work, manage a hub, a, a range of hubs around the country.

Mm-hmm. Um, I can't say that we have given any detailed consideration around changing how we do our compliance activities in terms of that space. Um, we are always looking at, is the way that we're delivering service or the way that we're delivering- Mm-hmm.... um, outcomes the best way for us to do that? But I can't say that we have done that in detail.

I don't think that we have. Have we, Andrew? No. I, I... Just one very quick follow-up. So I do recall, I think it was about three years ago, there was the Energy Hardship Conference here in Wellington. That's changed now. This year it was the Energy Affordability and Resilience Con- (laughs)... Conference because maybe there's no more hardship.

Um, but, um, but at that we did hear from MBIE that there was a, from the representative of MBIE that there was a, a, um, a need for better compliance, particularly in the South. And as we, as you, as you have explained already that, that, that work is happening. But I guess if it, if it hasn't progressed beyond that, do you think you could, will be capable of doing all of that with- in-house?

If it's just a... 'Cause I understood at the time that there was consideration of other ways of making that work. Mm-hmm. Uh, it's not something that we're presently considering. The, um, uh... I, I think before we would feel comfortable doing that, we'd need to have some assurance that there's some standards that those types of, um, assessors would follow, uh, which would be a policy-type question which Ministry of Housing and Urban Development would consider.

And I think they have previously considered that and chosen not to progress it. Um, as Suzanne said, we're always looking at how we can become more efficient and productive with our resources, and more, uh, ef- effective as well. Um, a- and, uh, we do that in a range of ways. We, as I mentioned earlier, we are really hot on using good data to understand where the highest harm is.

We have, over this past, um, or the previous financial year, uh, managed to s- sustain our target of 3,000 interventions. Just shy of 2,000 of those were done, uh, where we found w- w- were related to Healthy Home Standards matters, which is 100% uptick on the previous year. So we're constantly, um, adjusting where our focus is and, and helping our team prioritize where to put their effort so that it is directed to the, um, the, the harm that's happening in the market.

(coughs) So one of the things we do is we measure, uh, what we call the breach rate- Mm-hmm.... of when we go in and, and visit and, you know, do a compliance visit. And that's an indication of how well our intelligence is working and our data is working, so that when we're sending our compliance officers, um, to locations that we're actually targeting them in the right places at the right time.And what we're seeing is improvements in that as we utilize the data and insights that we have, um, to then enable us to go, yes, we're putting our resources not just blanket across, but actually where we

know we can actually make the biggest difference. So I think that's one of the things, um, how we measure that we're doing it well. Okay. And one very small one. There seem to be always one little extra one. (laughs) Um, the, um, the Energy Wellbeing Evaluation Network was established, um, I don't remember how many years ago, but MBIE was involved in that.

And I know that it, it has been really run out of the NGO sector without any government support, but, um, has been able to collate data. Mm-hmm. And if we're thinking about how we solve this challenge of energy hardship, understanding having the data, being able to evaluate is a really critical part.

Mm-hmm. Are you still involved with the Energy Wellbeing Evaluation Consortium? That's the term. Consortium. And, and, uh, and is this something that if you're not, or if you're not aware, is it something that wou- you'd be more interested in, in participating in? In assisting potentially to become more robust (inaudible) Yeah, I, um, I am aware that we have in the past been quite connected in with that group, and, um, understand we are still connected. It, um, but I would welcome the opportunity if that's a, a good, um,

avenue for us to be able to, uh, use information to make sure we're putting our, um, people, uh, directed to the right areas. Um, that kind of data exchange would be really helpful. Uh, so certainly when I get back to the office, ask the team to connect in again with the, with that consortium.

I'll just- Yeah. ... make one final- Yeah.... final, um- I guess it's a question, but I am aware that the Wellbeing Evaluation Consortium has struggled because it hasn't had any secretariat funding. Mm-hmm. Previously, that had been funded out of ECA and then a staff member moved over to MBIE, um, so it was in-kind re- resourcing, but that in-kind resourcing fell off and it's being held up by the community sector, which is already under incredible strain.

Do you think that there is a place here for government agency to chip in and to, to, to ensure that there is that resource? I think there's a value in it being inde- independent outside of government, but- (coughs)... there's a, there's a, there's a, um, a value in, in that data collection.

So, so we work with a number of NGOs and non-government agencies, um, through our market teams, whether it be in housing or in other areas. So that could be one of the ones that we look at, but where we get the best data, uh, for us, it's always around- (coughs)... best value that we get for the funds that we have that make the biggest o- Okay.

... impact and outcomes for, um, New Zealanders. So absolutely, we, we can always have a look at that in the mix of all the others that we work with. (laughs) All right. So, Arena Williams. Thank you. Um- Yeah.... just wanna bring you to the building and construction sector work that MBIE, um, facilitates in thinking about government (inaudible) (laughs)... um, and pretty extraordinary industry downturn.

20,000 construction sector job losses and I think today the statistic is 25% down for residential builds compared to last, uh, this, this time in 2022. Um, so my question is really around, is this the right time for this reform? Um, MBIE has a lot on with the sector, um, a number of consultations, and it might make sense to make changes on the supply side at some point to address, um, what's going on in the construction sector. But is it now?

Um, what, what feedback are you having from industry at the moment when they're, um, really crying out for government support, being asked, um, again and again about regulatory reform? Do you want to go Paul? Thanks. Yeah. I know you love to talk about (inaudible) (laughs) Well, tha- thanks. Good afternoon. Um, you're right that the, the sector's going through a, a, a challenging time now, right now, and a bit of a downturn. Um, although what we are seeing is that we are essentially returning to pre-COVID levels.

So we saw a really g- uh, a, a spike in construction activity, uh, post COVID, uh, when there was a period people weren't doing... weren't traveling or doing other things. Uh, we, um, are returning to more of a long run average in terms of the construction sector. The unfortunate nature of the construction sector is it does tend to operate in a, a little bit of a boom and bust cycle.

Uh, and we'd dearly love that to, to, to smooth out and to flatten out 'cause I don't think it works for, for anyone. It doesn't work for the industry. It doesn't work for those seeking to, to have construction undertaken. Um, on reform, um, I think the challenge is that there's never a great time to change things, uh, but there are always needs to have them changed.

The feedback we've received from the sector so far, and alwa- it's always gonna be slightly mixed, is that, uh, we've done quite a lot of consultation around the changes, and as you know, you've been involved through the committee being very broad based and there's quite a high degree of support for them, albeit that it's a lot of change going through. So some of the things we're trying to do, for example, in the building product space, is to make it easier to, uh, address a ra- a large number of, of products from overseas.

Uh, and I was going through, uh, this week just looking at the number of standards that we've approved. So we're not just making it up. We're approving international standards. Uh, similarly, um, things like the granny flats, which will make it easier and basically just stripping out some of the regulatory, uh, burden where it's not necessary. So there's always a, a quite a careful balance to be made about, you know, do you need it? Do you need it s- uh, to, for protection and for safety, et cetera? Or is it actually just having a, you know, uh, regulatory systems

that treat over time and you end up with rules that might have made sense at the time, but layered one over the top of each other don't...Um, and I think the next one will be... and this will probably be a really, you know, a, in many ways, a more substantive change, is the change to liability, uh- Mm-hmm.... regimes. Um, so that's not going to take place overnight, and that'll take place over a couple of years. Um, so, um, I appreciate the point that you're making around just the, the challenges for the sector at the moment.

But I, but I do think that actually pushing through... 'Cause some of these ideas have been around for a long time, and for, for a decade, and haven't actually g- got progressed. So, I think it is probably right to push through. I think th- that's really the question is, um, I think, uh, what, what you've said is w- what one would assume, that if these regulatory changes were making a difference for the sector, then th- that return to pre-COVID levels is what we'd see.

But, um, Westpac has today published its, um, impressions of the building sector, further signs the home building- Mm-hmm.... cycle has found the floor. Um, and they found, um, that the level of residential building activity remains around 25% lower than at its peak. Um, so it would suggest to me that at this time, where we've got these regulatory refor- reforms underway, that, as you say, things should be getting easier for the sector. They should be building granny flats.

They should be out there bringing in more cheaper and more affordable product. In fact, um, we're still seeing a lag. So, what is MBIE doing to, um, make sure that, like the UK's house building sector lagged behind (audio cuts out) by six years, that New Zealand won't be in the same situation?

So, I think some of the other... Th- th- this does go to the nature of the cyc- well, supply and demand aspects for, for, for residential homes in particular. And I think there's been some other commentary, uh, of course, last week from, it might have been Westpac, don't quote me on that. (laughs) Which was reflecting upon, uh, why residential prices were still relatively low, you know, far down from the peak.

Uh, and they were saying, well, actually, a couple of reasons for that. Uh, firstly, there had been such a, a, a peak in, in supply, and, uh, the comment you made is from the peak, and that post-COVID peak was, was really high. Uh, which meant that there were a lot more houses built. Uh, that combined with, uh, low interest rates, uh, and just concerns, it means that people are being quite cautious.

So, what we're not seeing at the moment is an under-supply of housing. Mm-hmm. Uh, we're seeing actually that there's, there's maybe not quite an equilibrium. But there's a bit more housing than we have had for many years. That's a good thing. Mm-hmm. Uh, but it does mean that the, the building cycle will kind of lag a bit as that cycle of supply and demand, uh, is matched.

So, I still think that we, you know, the, if you look at the figures, and I think it was about, we're about 2017, 2018 levels of consents, going back to that. It really did peak in '21, '22. Uh, and that was probably an unsustainable level. And what you saw when that was happening was really high levels of, uh, price inflation, uh, in the building construction sector, uh, and labor supply, uh, challenges, and you couldn't get things built.

Mm-hmm. So, that kind of heat in the market's not good- Mm-hmm.... either. Okay. A supplementary for Suzanne. Uh, thank you. Um, just, um, in regards to the building and construction sector, the government's got a, um, a broad work plan in this area with, um, changes, uh, not only to products, um, coming in, but also consenting arrangements. Um, is MBIE keeping up with all of those changes, and, um, within your teams, and then also how are you measuring your own, um, impact within the system?

Um, MBIE... (laughs) We're running to keep up. It's a, it's a, it's a really... It's a comprehensive reform program. Yeah. Mm-hmm. Um, and, you know, with... I mean, I've got to say, I'm super proud of the teams and the work they've done. Uh, if I could do a, a slight skite, uh, they did win the, uh, the Spirit of Service Award for Public Policy this year and the Prime Minister's Award for, for Spirit of Service to Government-... which is, which is great. Really reflected the work they had done.

Um, I think we've... Uh, my observation is, uh, that they've done a really good job of keeping... A- and actually keeping up with the policy and providing the kind of advice you want the public service to provide- Mm-hmm.... free and frank advice from cost, benefit, risks, challenges. And that, that's a good thing. Mm-hmm. Uh, ministers will sometimes agree and sometimes take different views.

That's their job as well. Mm-hmm. Um, and I think, you know, that the work program they delivered has been great. Mm-hmm. And I think will be very impactful for New Zealand. So that, that's a, that's a great thing. Um, in terms of that impact though, uh, given the nature of these reforms are really substantive, some of those impacts will take some time, some time to come through. Mm-hmm. Some of the things that you're seeing, like the simple things around granny flats- Mm-hmm.

... you'd expect that to happen relatively quickly. Uh, things like proportionate liability will fundamentally change the, the risk p- uh, uh, profile for the sector. And our expectation is that that will change behavior- Yeah. Mm-hmm.... and the speed of getting things done. Um, you know, other things like earthquake prone buildings, for example- Yeah.

... I think will have a, uh, quite immediate and significant impact for large numbers of people. I mean, th- that's been a very challenging piece of policy to work through, you know, 'cause its genesis was, you know, post-Christchurch and trying to make, get the balance of protection versus, uh, cost benefit. So I think some of those things, and we know from discussions with people who have been affected by the earthquake, prime buildings for example, that the amount of stress, uh, and challenge for some of those, uh-

(coughs) ... people have been really high. Yes. Uh, and we were seeing levels of non-compliance because it wasn't able to be complied with. Mm-hmm. So, for example, we've, we've estimated this is going to take about $8 billion of cost, um, out. And taking Auckland out, for example. (clears throat) Yeah. You know, the, the risk in Auckland is probably far more volcanic than, than seismic, uh, and yet, uh, 40% of the cost from the earthquake prime building regime was, was located in Auckland.

Mm-hmm. Um, so look, really proud to have, to have the teams involved in this. I do want to call out, as I know Suzanne did this morning- (laughs)... just, just their commitment and work. Yeah. Uh, and, uh, you'd expect that everyone's a bit tired at this time of year, um, and you'd almost be- Not as good as I feel. ... surprised that they weren't. But they do- I think that- They are also very positive about what they've achieved so far.

Absolutely. So that keeps you going. It's great to be able to do stuff that, that actually, you know, is going to impact on people's lives positively. Mm-hmm. That's wonderful. So, are you saying that, um, your- you could measure your impact by the, um, the economic benefits that we're seeing, um, as being one of the main, um, measures? In which case there's straightaway $8 billion, um, and all of the others. So that's- Yeah. ... quite a substantial return on whatever investment it would- was required.

I- I- I think we'd- we'd- we'd seek to measure impact on a range of factors. Uh, economic obviously is an important one. We are-... an economic agency. Uh, you know, some of the things we've done, for example, in the building products space, uh, is to expand the market, uh, and, you know, reduce costs and increase choice for consumers. Mm-hmm. Uh, the $8 billion is- is another case.

But actually, economics is a proxy measure for- Yes. ... people and their experiences as well- Mm-hmm.... and the cost for them for- for doing building, how easy it is to- to buy a house and things. So yeah, economics is a proxy for that, but at the end of the day, it's how it impacts on people. Okay. Any more questions on this topic? Yeah, Anne Williams. Um, just going to the granny flats, I agree with you that that's something you might expect to see soon. Um, and I wanted to ask around the modeling that MBIE, um,

did and the advice they provided, um, to government and helpfully to the committee that dealt with the bill, um, because those numbers moved around and I want to know what you're seeing now as actual numbers. I think the initial, um, estimate of the number of granny flats that would be built was somewhere around 7,000 and then by the end of, uh, the committee's hearings, we were considering 13,000.

Yeah. Um, that makes a big difference, especially if they're grouped up in somewhere like Auckland. Um, what are you seeing coming through the system at the moment? What are your expectations based on how the market has responded to that, about the uptake? Okay, so I'll have to come back to you on the specific numbers. Oh, it's okay. Yeah. I- I don't need the specific numbers, but I- I'm asking you order of magnitude. Okay, so I think what- what we've seen is quite a lot of interest in it.

As you know, through the committee process, uh, there was quite a lot of fine-tuning about what the nature- Mm-hmm.... of those provisions would be, the- the relationship to... 'cause it wasn't just the building, uh, the code, it was also about how the RM system worked with that as well. Um, so I think through that we- we basically fine-tuned and the committee fine-tuned, you know, just exactly where were the risky things. Uh, we made some changes, for example, that would allow and were about to allow, uh, uh, wet areas to be

fitted within, uh, gr- granny flats. That required a specific different change 'cause there are some risks around, uh, flat wet- wet areas. Um, we can come back with the actual numbers. There's been quite a lot of interest, I- but I don't know exactly how much building taking place to date. Just what about... Um, MBIE did a lot of advice and con- consultation with the sector in this last year around, um, allowing granny flats to be built on Maori land and enabling papakainga- Yeah. Mm-hmm, yeah.

... that was missing from the final legislation. Um, w- what is MBIE doing to ensure that that work and that goodwill that there is out there for Maori land to have granny flats associated with either dwellings or marae, um, isn't just lost or- or parked on the sort of policy thinking stage?

So, we have a limited role in going out and- and doing, uh, I suppose marketing of- of this is the new thing that you can do. We've made sure it's very freely available. Um, I think the- the- the point that you're raising, and it's a really good one, is, you know, what channels can we use to make sure that people know what they can now do? Um, so we'd expect both the BCAs but also, um, and I think this is probably something we haven't done but maybe we should, is reaching out through, uh, iwi groups and saying, "Look, just want to make sure that

you are aware that you can now do this on papakainga," et cetera, and it's, you know, that's more liberalized. So I think that's probably something we should follow up on. I- I just- I think the question is that, um, of the major dwellings that are- that allow for, um, gr- granny flats to be built alongside them, marae is not one. Mm-hmm. But that doesn't seem to have been what MBIE consulted on.

Um, but the legislative outcome is that you cannot build these as papakainga associated with a marae, you must have a major dwelling. Yeah, and I think, um, the major dwelling... 'Cause the marae, as you know, is- is not a household unit. Uh, so that was the- the policy genesis of it. I think, you know, it was considered, um, but it's- the- the government decided that they wanted to stick with the major dwellings.

Right, that's a shame. Um, just on the... Also a question about the, uh, earthquake prone buildings and- Yeah.... yeah, absolutely agree with you and, um, shout- a shout out to your team about what is a very complicated area of policy work and consultation with stakeholders. Um, the, um, decision around the exclusion of Auckland, um, you've said is related to the risk for, um, vol- volcanic risk but not necessarily earthquakes. I think, um, the quote that remains with most politicians I would say that, uh, when the mayor was talking

about, you know, the last earthquake in Auckland was 70,000 years ago. Um, why, uh, though when volcanoes are associated with seismic risk do now none of the standards, um, recognize what you- you've rightly pointed to as the volcanic risk? So, um, we took advice, uh, from Earth Sciences New Zealand as they're now called and GNS around the seismic risk areas in New Zealand, and I think there are about three or four areas that have differential seismic risk.

So, uh, Auckland and, uh, that part of the country had the very, very lowest seismic risk. Uh, Wellington, as you would expect, had high seismic risk. Canterbury was moderate. Otago was actually higher than we had expected, so that moved into- into a slightly higher seismic risk zone. Um, so it- it's true that, um, if you're in the- the, uh, you know, New Zealand is a geologically young country and it is seismically active, but within that space, uh, the Auckland area was advised to us by the, uh, geological scientists as being the lowest risk.

So it wasn't associat- the volcano was really meant- meant to be a- a counterpoint about there's a number of risks that people face in everyday life. In the Auckland space, the advice that we received was that the seismic risk was relatively low compared to the rest of the country. It, I mean, that is my question. There are 53 volcanoes in Auckland. Yeah. Volcanoes make buildings shake.

Why do we not have rules now in place in Auckland that are designed to upgrade buildings so that when they shake they don't...... uh, cause, cause damage. So, I mean, the, the building code does include in it, you know, the, the rules that you have to do to make sure it's solid and firm, et cetera. The problem with the earthquake prone regime, and, as you'll know, is (clears throat) that it was, uh, predicated on the fact that any one element of the building could result in an MBS, which would be, you know- Mm-hmm.

... perhaps lower than 30- Mm-hmm. ... even if it was a very simple thing. In addressing, uh, a more, uh, risk-based approach to, uh, the regulations around that, we did two things. Uh, we looked at the likely seismic re- 'cause, 'cause risk mitigation is always a likelihood impact. Um (clears throat) , so we looked at, uh, likelihood on seismic regimes and again, the advice that we received from the scientists was, in a seismic, uh, risk area, Auckland was relatively low.

Uh, and then you look at, uh, what the, the consequence is, uh, so we basically said there's not a major issue in Auckland, but in other, uh, other areas, there, there is a risk, and that risk is largest with, uh, buildings that are greater than three stories, unreinforced masonry, or, you know, heavily concrete ones. Mm-hmm. Right. Mm-hmm. Right. I'll ask a question about consumer rights.

So I was just looking at, uh, the numbers and it shows that, uh, it's 55% of people, uh, a- and this is adult consumers, those who know at least a moderate amount about their rights as a consumer. Uh, this is a 10% increase compared to last measurement in 2022, 2023, but still it looks quite low, 55%. Uh, I mean, w- what is the issue there, that it's only 55% of adult consumers know what their rights are as consumers?

I, I think it is, uh, it's always the complexities of, you know, trying to explain a broad brush of, um, legislation to consumers around what can they, what can they do via, in the various pieces of legislation there are. For us and the small team that we have in terms of in that space, where, where we have managed- (coughs)... and I have to say that 10% is quite an achievement to, to lift that amount of awareness through a population of, you know, 5.3 million, um, is around us making sure that we are using the best channels we can in terms of

communication. What we're, what we do when we measure that awareness level is we do set ourselves a reasonably high bar about, you know, when consumers see that aware, it's not just that aware, "Oh, can I take my product back?" And they have an awareness of what their rights are in terms of under the Consumer Guarantees Act. So to get to that level is relatively good, um, believe it or not. I, obviously I would love it to be 100%, but I don't think we'll, we'll always be there. What we would really look to seek to do is that

consumers know where to go to find that advice. So even if they don't know it in the moment, "What am I allowed to do? I've got this..." for like, it's just, and this is just an example, not a real life example. "If I've got this fridge I've just bought and it doesn't work after I've sent the... How do I take it? But, you know, what can I do? Can I take it back? Do I have to do this? Do I have to do that? What are my rights?" What we need to know is that they can go to a well-known safe place to get that advice, and that's where we've got to ensure that government information

is up there and set and lights for them so that they don't need to know everything about the legislation in their heads- Mm-hmm. ... as much as my colleagues know. Mm-hmm. Um, I would probably phone them first, but if not, you know where to go. And so- Mm-hmm.... I think that's where our consumer protection information, the awareness campaigns that we run in those spaces, stuff about product safety is to try and find, if you're unsure, at least know where to go.

Mm-hmm. Um, I'm not sure how many calls we take on that. We take quite a few calls and on most occasion, uh, most days around, uh, people looking for their, trying to understand some more about what they can do. Um, I think last year we did about 198 recalls we would have published and pro- just under the 200, doesn't include the recent.

I've, um, got included the recent as well. Um, but it is a constant ongoing piece of work for us to try and lift that level of understanding so that consumers are on a fair play- field. Mm-hmm. You know, and they understand what they can do and that- Yeah.... they have rights. Yeah. So what was done- Mm-hmm.... uh, like, um, differently, uh, between 2022 to 2023 to 2024, 2025, to have this increase of 10%? Because it, it was consistent before that, uh, 2020 and 2021 report had 45%- Mm-hmm.

... then 2022 and 2023 had 45 and now it's 55. So what happened in this period that you got this increase? So I guess one of the things we have done is lean much more into proactive media in that space. Um, you'll have probably seen we, we have a, a, it's almost an, a celebrity spokesperson now, um, Ian Caplan who's on TV every, it feels like every five minutes talking about consumer rights. Um, we have proactively gone with our comms team to get ourselves when there is an issue to use the pulpit that MBIE has, um, to get

access to media to talk to, and the, the public in, through programs that we know they are listening to- Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. ... um, and also through social media channels that we know would be. So I think what you're seeing is the result of our targeting, the improved targeting we have- Mm-hmm.... and being actually more, let's see, um, leaning in and being more planned and purposeful around how we're using those communication channels and how we measure the impact of those channels too.
